title: OpenAI overhauls ChatGPT memory with new Dreaming V3 architecture
subtitle: Background synthesis replaces the saved-memories list; rollout to Plus and Pro began June 4, with Free-tier expansion enabled by a 5x cut in serving cost.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI began rolling out Dreaming V3, an overhauled memory system for ChatGPT, to Plus and Pro subscribers in the United States on June 4, 2026. The new system replaces the saved-memories list that users could edit by hand. In its place is a background process that synthesizes what to remember from conversation itself, updates older memories as context shifts, and lets stale information fade. Free-tier expansion is queued for late July, made possible by what OpenAI says is a roughly 5x reduction in the serving cost of each dreaming pass.

Memory has been the weakest part of ChatGPT for two years. Users would either dump everything into the saved-memories list and watch the model contradict itself, or turn memory off entirely and re-explain context every session. Dreaming V3 is OpenAI's attempt to move memory from a flat key-value store to something closer to how a human remembers — selectively, with decay, and without explicit instruction. OpenAI says the system optimizes along three axes: freshness, continuity, and relevance.

Persistent memory is the contested frontier across all major chat products. Anthropic shipped project-scoped memory in Claude earlier in the year, and Google has been quietly testing cross-session recall inside Gemini Workspace. The technical bet differs by lab — Anthropic favors explicit, user-visible memory blocks, while OpenAI is going the opposite direction, hiding the mechanism and letting the model decide. Both approaches have legitimate failure modes. Which one wins likely comes down to whether users trust an opaque process more than they hate maintaining a list.

A note for learners: if you use ChatGPT for serious work, take fifteen minutes after Dreaming V3 reaches you to audit what it has stored. The interface still lets you view, edit, and delete memories — it just no longer requires you to. Treat that audit the way you'd treat reviewing browser history: not paranoid, just hygiene.
